employer_type.llc_must_note_if_file_as_partnership_c_o: LLC taxed as S corporation for federal income tax purposes

taxable_waiver_compensation.if_yes_are_the_benefits_paid_per_pay_or_: The additional taxable compensation is paid on a per-pay-period basis. For full-time employees who are eligible for the group medical plan but elect to waive coverage, we currently provide a taxable opt-out credit of $95.00 per biweekly pay period. For part-time employees who meet eligibility thresholds and waive coverage, the amount is $45.00 per biweekly pay period. We chose per-pay distribution rather than a lump sum so that it aligns closely with ongoing payroll, is easier for employees to understand in the context of each paycheck, and reduces the risk of having to recoup overpaid amounts if an employee’s status changes mid-year. The opt-out credit appears as a separate taxable earning line item on each paycheck and is included in W-2 wages at year end. We do not offer a lump-sum option at this time, because our payroll system and internal controls are set up around regular, recurring payments rather than large one-time disbursements.
taxable_waiver_compensation.if_yes_what_are_the_conditions_required_:
To receive the extra taxable compensation for waiving group health coverage, employees must meet several specific conditions that we communicate in our annual open enrollment materials and new hire orientation packets:

1. **Eligibility for the group health plan:**
   - The employee must be classified as a benefits-eligible employee under our internal policy (generally regularly scheduled to work at least 30 hours per week for full-time, or at least 25 hours per week for part-time benefits-eligible status).
   - The employee must have completed any applicable waiting period (currently the first of the month following 60 days of employment) and be otherwise eligible to enroll in the company-sponsored group medical plan.

2. **Proof of other qualifying coverage:**
   - The employee must provide documentation that they are covered under another qualifying health plan (for example, coverage through a spouse’s employer, a parent’s plan, TRICARE, or a government exchange plan). Acceptable documentation includes a copy of the other plan’s ID card, a confirmation of coverage letter, or an enrollment confirmation screenshot with the employee’s name and effective date.
   - The proof must be submitted at initial eligibility or during open enrollment, and any time the alternate coverage changes materially (e.g., termination of the spouse’s plan).

3. **Annual election during open enrollment or initial eligibility:**
   - The employee must affirmatively elect to waive our group health coverage and elect the taxable opt-out credit on the appropriate enrollment or waiver form.
   - Elections are generally irrevocable for the plan year unless the employee experiences a qualifying life event (QLE), such as marriage, divorce, birth or adoption of a child, or loss of other coverage.

4. **No use of HRA or HSA funds tied to the company plan:**
   - Employees receiving the opt-out credit cannot simultaneously participate in any employer-sponsored Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA) or company-funded Health Savings Account (HSA) that is conditioned on enrollment in our group medical plan. If they later elect our medical plan and become HSA-eligible, the opt-out credit stops and HSA contributions start based on their new election.

5. **Active employment and payroll status:**
   - The employee must be actively employed and receiving wages in the applicable pay period. The opt-out credit is not paid during unpaid leaves of absence, long-term disability periods with no wages, or after termination of employment.
   - If an employee’s scheduled hours drop below the benefits-eligible threshold for more than one full pay period, the company reserves the right to discontinue the opt-out credit effective the beginning of the next pay period.

6. **Timely notification of status changes:**
   - Employees must notify HR within 30 days if they lose their other coverage or if any family or employment status changes that would affect eligibility occur (for example, divorce from the spouse whose plan they are on, or if the spouse’s employer stops offering coverage).
   - If an employee fails to notify HR and continues receiving the opt-out credit despite losing other coverage, we may stop the payments retroactively to the date of loss of coverage and adjust future payrolls accordingly.

7. **Acknowledgment of tax treatment and no guarantee of future payments:**
   - Employees must sign an acknowledgment that the opt-out credit is fully taxable income, not a reimbursement, and that it will increase their taxable wages for federal, state, and local income tax purposes as well as FICA.
   - The acknowledgment also clarifies that the program is subject to change or discontinuation at the company’s discretion, typically aligned with the plan year.

In practice, HR (primarily me, Melissa Davenport, and our HR coordinator) keeps a checklist with copies of the waiver form, proof of other coverage, and the signed acknowledgment for each employee who receives the opt-out credit. We review these records at each open enrollment period to ensure that every employee receiving the taxable waiver compensation still meets all of the above conditions.

authorization__referral.signature_1: Melissa J. Davenport, HR & Benefits Manager
authorization__referral.who_referred_you_to_us_please_note_name_: Referred by our external benefits broker: Daniel R. McKenzie, Senior Benefits Consultant, FrontRange Benefits Group. Email: dmckenzie@frontrangebenefits.com. Phone: (720) 555-3174. Daniel has been working with us since late 2019 on our medical, dental, vision, and ancillary benefits strategy, and he recommended formalizing and updating our Section 125 Premium Only Plan documentation as part of our 2026 renewal process to ensure compliance with current IRS requirements and to align our cafeteria plan language with our actual payroll and enrollment practices.
